gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: Just rode in from the front. Still have Asculum mud on my boots — I buried three centurions in it this morning. The war's going badly, Crispus. Turns out the Italians fight like Romans. Funny, that. We're the ones who taught them.

responses.playerDialogue: Skip the eulogy. How bad — numbers, positions, the real picture.
If the war's going badly, someone in this city is already calculating how to profit from it.
What's the command structure saying? Is there an actual plan, or just speeches?
I'm sorry about your centurions. Were they good men?

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: Sit. Drink. Tell me what happened out there.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: Two legions broken in Samnium. Lupus dead — half his army with him at the Tolenus. The Marsi hit us before dawn and we never found the line again. Back here, the senate debates who deserves a triumph for victories that haven't bloody happened. Meanwhile every Italian town from here to Brundisium is sharpening iron. We need bodies, Crispus — trained ones. I've got authority to write military commissions. So — you want to do something, or keep having opinions about it?

options.playerDialogue: What about Sulla? Word is he's actually winning down in Campania.
I'm more dangerous behind the lines — supply, logistics, intelligence. Where's the machine breaking down?

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: The best. One of them — Sextus — survived the Cimbri. Fifteen years carrying a shield for Rome. Killed outside Asculum by an Italian who'd been his tentmate in Numidia. That's what this war is. Brothers butchering brothers because the senate couldn't bring itself to share a gods-damned ballot. But I didn't ride back here to mourn. I need people who can help. The front needs officers. Rome needs someone watching the supply lines. Which one are you?

options.playerDialogue: The front. I didn't come this far to weigh grain sacks.
What about Sulla? Word is he's actually winning down in Campania.
The supply lines. A war runs on bread and iron before it runs on courage. Put me where the logistics are rotting.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: Good man. I'll write you in as military tribune, attached to the populares levies — you'll serve under Marian officers. Pay's irregular, food's worse, and the wine tastes like something a mule refused. But you'll command a century, and when this mess is over, every man in Rome will know you stood on the line. The veterans will call you one of their own. Trust me — that's worth more than any senatorial handshake.

options.playerDialogue: I'll serve. But I want to know who I'm really bleeding for — Rome, or one old man's pride.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: Sulla. Yes. He's not just competent — he's winning his whole sector while the rest of us bleed into the dirt. Took Stabiae, shoved the Samnites back from Pompeii. His men worship him because he wins and splits the loot. That's the Marian model, ironically — Sulla learned it from the old man and now he's better at it. Don't repeat that. Point is, when this war ends, Sulla will have a loyal army and a reputation the senate can't touch. That should scare the piss out of everyone.

options.playerDialogue: Noted. Terrifying. Now — what can I actually do on the Marian side?

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Smart. The supply chain from Ostia to the southern front is a catastrophe — grain shipments late, equipment sent to the wrong camps, half the quartermasters skimming off the top like it's a gods-given right. I'll commission you as staff officer. You'll have access to the whole logistics web and authority to crack skulls when the numbers don't add up. Less glory, more impact. The men who keep an army fed win more wars than the ones swinging gladii.

options.playerDialogue: I'll take it. Point me at the worst of it.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: Dawn. Pack light — one tunic, your blade, and whatever the gods gave you for guts. Your century's mustering at the Porta Capena. They're green, but the Italians will educate them fast enough. A week from now you'll have your first taste of the line. When you come back — and you will come back, because I don't befriend men who die easily — you'll be someone different.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: Honest question — deserves an honest answer. Both. Right now they're the same thing. Marius built this army, and this army is the only wall between Rome and a hundred thousand angry Italians. When the war's over, maybe that changes. But today? You fight for the man on your left and the man on your right. That's all any soldier ever fights for. The rest is speeches.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: Capua. The depot there is the bottleneck — fix Capua and you fix half the southern front. I'll send a rider ahead so they know you're coming. And Crispus — don't let the quartermasters stall you. Half of them are on the take and the other half are covering for the first half. Use your authority. That's what it's bloody for.

gaius_norbanus_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: Your call. The war ends with or without you. But when it's over, the men who didn't serve are going to find the peace very cold. Military credibility matters in this city — always has. Without it, you're just another civilian with a toga and an opinion.

operations.reason: Volunteered for front-line service under Marian command

deltas.reason: Chose combat when safer options were available

operations.reason: Supporting the Marian war effort from the supply lines

deltas.reason: Chose the practical work of logistics over front-line glory
